title: Remarks on the Economic Outlook and Monetary Policy
summary: Musalem reads the economy as on solid footing with a healthy labor market and supportive financial conditions, but flags recent softness in consumer spending and housing as a downside risk to growth. He sees inflation still above the 2% target with risks skewed to the upside, and judges current policy as only modestly restrictive after the fall's 100bp of cuts. He argues for a patient hold: modestly restrictive policy should persist until there is confidence inflation is converging to 2%, with further easing warranted only if the labor market deteriorates while inflation and expectations behave. His core preoccupation is keeping medium- to longer-term inflation expectations well anchored, drawing on the 1970s versus post-2022 disinflation episodes to argue that anchored expectations are what make a balanced response to conflicting goals feasible.

key_quotes.quote: If the economy remains strong and inflation remains above target, then I believe modestly restrictive policy will continue to be appropriate until there is confidence inflation is converging to the FOMC's 2% target.
key_quotes.context: First of his four key messages, on the conditions for holding policy modestly restrictive.

key_quotes.quote: Thus, I perceive the risks to inflation as skewed to the upside and am watching near- and longer-term inflation expectations carefully.
key_quotes.context: Assessment of risks in the baseline scenario discussion, citing above-potential growth and full employment.

key_quotes.quote: Policy is now modestly restrictive and meaningfully less restrictive than it was seven months ago.
key_quotes.context: Characterizing the current stance after the fall's 100bp of cuts.

key_quotes.quote: I believe a patient approach now will help us as we seek maximum employment, price stability and a durable economic expansion.
key_quotes.context: Stating his near-term policy inclination given two-sided risks.
